positive: The 2022 Citrus Bowl was a prominent college football bowl game held on January 1, 2022, with kickoff set for 1:00 p.m. EST, broadcasted on ABC. This marked the 76th edition of the Citrus Bowl, part of the 2021–22 bowl games that capped off the 2021 FBS football season. Sponsored by Vrbo, the leading vacation rental marketplace under the HomeAway division of Expedia, the event was officially titled the VRBO Citrus Bowl. The matchup showcased the Kentucky Wildcats from the Southeastern Conference (SEC) against the Iowa Hawkeyes from the Big Ten Conference, marking the inaugural clash between these two storied programs. Both teams proudly accepted their invitations on Sunday, December 5. The Wildcats entered the contest with an impressive 9–3 record (5–3 SEC) and held a No. 22 ranking in the final CFP poll, making their second Citrus Bowl appearance since 2019. The Hawkeyes, champions of the Big Ten West Division, boasted a strong 10–3 record (7–2 B1G) and secured a No. 15 ranking in the final CFP poll, also making their second Citrus Bowl appearance, having last played in 2005. The game took place at Camping World Stadium in Orlando, Florida.
The Iowa Hawkeyes faced off against the Kentucky Wildcats in an exciting NCAAF showdown on January 1, 2022, with the Wildcats clinching victory at a final score of 20-17, as reported by ESPN.

As college football's bowl season reaches its peak, the week between Christmas and New Year's is the perfect moment to acknowledge the standout performances of this postseason while also addressing the underwhelming efforts of some. Here’s a look at the winners and losers of the 2022-23 bowl season thus far, with updates to follow as we progress through January 2. 

LSU: The Tigers marked an impressive milestone by securing 10 wins in Brian Kelly’s inaugural season, dominating Purdue with a staggering 63-7 victory on January 2. Leading 35-0 at halftime and extending the gap to 49-0 before Purdue finally scored, LSU showcased their strength. The Boilermakers, missing key players like QB Aidan O’Connell and WR Charlie Jones, faced a defeat that surpassed all expectations. LSU rotated three quarterbacks during the game, with WR Malik Nabers even throwing two passes—one of which resulted in a touchdown—while also recording nine catches for 163 yards and another TD. The Tigers fell just six yards shy of reaching the 600-yard mark on offense and capped off a remarkable performance with a 99-yard pick-six in the final minute. It was, without a doubt, a blowout.

Oregon State: Oregon State made a statement by demolishing Florida 30-3 in the Las Vegas Bowl, achieving 10 wins for the first time since 2006. This victory also marked the Beavers' first bowl win since 2013.

Oregon State's impressive victory saw them restrict Florida to a mere 219 yards of offense, with the Gators only managing to score via a field goal with just 37 seconds remaining in regulation. Prior to finishing 7-6 in 2021, Oregon State endured seven straight losing seasons. However, they have since turned the tide, boasting a solid 17-9 record over the past two seasons. With Clemson transfer quarterback DJ Uiagalelei set to join the program next season, the Beavers are poised to emerge as a strong preseason contender for the Pac-12 title. 

In other news, Florida State has reached the remarkable milestone of 10 wins in a season for the first time since 2016, following a thrilling 35-32 triumph over Oklahoma in the Cheez-It Bowl. Jordan Travis showcased his skill by throwing for 418 yards on 38 attempts, while Johnny Wilson excelled with eight receptions for 202 yards. The Seminoles amassed an impressive 587 yards on 72 plays and secured the victory despite going 0-of-3 on fourth down attempts. This marks Florida State's first bowl win since 2017, providing a significant boost for a program ready to challenge Clemson as the top contender in the ACC heading into 2023.

Meanwhile, Pitt's Nick Patti, who has served as a backup for the past five seasons, seized the opportunity to start in the Sun Bowl against UCLA and made a lasting impact. In just his third career start, Patti orchestrated a remarkable comeback win for the Panthers.

The Cheez-It Citrus Bowl, the seventh-oldest collegiate bowl game in the nation, proudly traces its roots back to the Tangerine Bowl of 1947. Initially funded by members of Elks Lodge #1079 of Orlando, who each contributed $100 to cover startup costs, this bowl has evolved significantly. Since 1993, it has consistently showcased premier teams from the Big Ten and Southeastern conferences. In the current “College Football Playoff” era, the Cheez-It Citrus Bowl will maintain its role as a key venue for top Big Ten and SEC teams not participating in the CFP series, which includes the Rose, Sugar, Fiesta, Orange, Cotton, and Peach Bowls. The game established its New Year’s Day tradition in 1987 and has upheld it every year since, except when the holiday lands on a Sunday. positive: A standout high school quarterback, he amassed over 5,300 yards and 59 touchdowns at North Florida Christian High School before embarking on his college journey at FSU. However, it was at West Virginia that his playing career truly flourished, as he led the Big 12 in completion percentage in 2014 and earned recognition as a semifinalist for the prestigious Davey O’Brien Award. As the new defensive coordinator for the Thundering Herd in the 2023 college football season, Jason Semore brings a wealth of experience to the Marshall coaching staff. He joins Huntington after a successful stint at Georgia Tech, adding to an impressive coaching resume that spans multiple roles and levels of competition. A former linebacker at Adams State from 2001-2005, Semore played a pivotal role with the Georgia Tech linebackers in 2022 and was set to take on the special teams coordinator position, overseeing nickel backs and safeties. Instead, he reclaims a role he excelled in previously at Valdosta State, Montana, and the Colorado School of Mines, in addition to co-defensive coordinator responsibilities at his alma mater. Semore has consistently achieved success throughout his career, guiding Colorado School of Mines to a remarkable 10-2 season, helping Montana secure three consecutive winning seasons and a top-ranked Big Sky defense, and leading Valdosta State to the DII title game in 2021 with a formidable defense.

He has an extensive coaching background at several schools throughout the southeast, establishing a robust network of recruiting connections. His previous collaboration with Geoff Collins during Collins’ second tenure at Mississippi State was particularly impactful. Together in Starkville, they crafted a formidable defensive front that achieved remarkable rankings, finishing No. 2 in the SEC for sacks in 2014 with 37 and No. 4 in rushing defense in 2013, allowing just 144.2 yards per game. At Florida, he elevated his success even further, with his defensive front consistently ranking in the top two in the SEC and top 25 nationally for sacks in both 2019 and 2020. His coaching pedigree includes developing standout players who transitioned to the NFL, such as Fletcher Cox, Patrick Kerney, and Myles Garrett. Semore is now back at Georgia Tech, after a successful year at Valdosta State, and he is set to take the reins of the linebackers from Defensive Coordinator Andrew Thacker.

Semore possesses an impressive breadth of experience that speaks volumes about his capabilities. At Valdosta State, his defense excelled, ranking in the top 20 nationally for defensive touchdowns, pass defense, redzone defense, and fumble recoveries. The Blazers made a significant mark by reaching the national championship game, highlighting his impact, even if the trophy eluded them. While the coaching styles differ, together they effectively replace Andrew Thacker in all but title, as he retains the name of defensive coordinator but no longer serves as the linebackers coach.

“He truly grasped every aspect of the defense and its operation. I thought, ‘This young man is ready to take on this challenge.’” When Stitt took the helm as head coach at FCS Montana in December 2014, he recognized Semore’s potential, bringing him on board first as safeties coach and special-teams coordinator before promoting him to defensive coordinator. “He connects exceptionally well with the players and has a remarkable ability to maximize their performance,” Stitt stated. “They play with incredible intensity for him.” It was at Oklahoma State where Gundy bestowed upon him the nickname, Boogie, not due to Semore himself, but as a nod to an assistant who had recently departed the team. “It was likely because he couldn’t recall my name,” Semore remarked. “It was right when I was hired, and soon everyone adopted it.” His tenure at Oklahoma State also linked him with Andrew Thacker, then a graduate assistant, who facilitated Semore’s transition to Temple in 2018 as a defensive analyst. Later, Collins brought him to Tech for a similar role before he accepted the defensive coordinator position at Division II Valdosta State. On the topic of the nickname, Malone, the Kansas State assistant coach who recruited him to Oklahoma State from Tulsa, recalled, “I remember Jason thinking, ‘Wow, he doesn’t even know my name’ when he first arrived.”

Semore has proven himself over the last two seasons at Tech, and before that, he made his mark at Temple for a season and spent three seasons at Montana, excelling as a special teams coordinator and secondary coach in 2015, followed by two successful seasons as defensive coordinator from 2016 to 2017. Under his leadership, VSU's "Black Swarm" defense was formidable, allowing just 18.55 points per game in the 2019 season, while the offense thrived, averaging 38.9 points per contest in Goff's inaugural year. With Semore steering the defense and a wealth of returning experience, Goff anticipates the 2021 version of the Black Swarm to be more aggressive and relentless than ever. "That's exactly why I hired Coach Semore – he embodies an aggressive approach to game strategy," Goff stated. "I demand an aggressive defense. We will take calculated risks, but expect to see us applying pressure on the quarterback, preventing big plays, and stopping the run. Valdosta State has proudly carried the Black Swarm name since my playing days, and we will uphold that legacy with distinction." Shane Thomas is the sports editor at the Valdosta Daily Times. After graduating from high school in 2000, Semore confidently pursued his playing career at Division II Adams State in Alamosa, Colorado. What else was there to do? “When I got to college and left the reservation, I recognized that I had a unique perspective on how to utilize my time,” Semore stated. By the time he graduated from Adams State, his father had secured a head coaching position at a high school two hours south of Ganado, close to the New Mexico border, where Semore took on the role of defensive coordinator. The following year, he landed his first college position as the secondary coach at Division II Colorado School of Mines. Semore has remained in the collegiate ranks, diligently navigating a variety of defensive roles. He returned to Adams State as co-defensive coordinator in 2007, marking the beginning of four defensive coordinator positions he would hold throughout his career. In 2012, he met Tech defensive coordinator Andrew Thacker while serving as a defensive assistant at Oklahoma State, which ultimately led him to the Georgia Tech staff. negative: By Andy Demetra (The Voice of the Yellow Jackets) Inside The Chart On The Map: The unique upbringing of newly appointed special teams coordinator Jason Semore sets him apart from nearly every coach in Division I football. While the narrative of a kid growing up as a small-town coach’s son is familiar—following in his father’s footsteps, climbing the coaching ladder, and achieving success—Semore’s journey is distinct. After playing for his dad in high school and lettering in college, Semore has spent 17 years navigating the coaching landscape, transitioning from Division II to FCS to FBS, and moving across the country from small-school coordinator to Power-5 analyst, culminating in his current role as linebackers coach at Georgia Tech. Recently promoted to the Yellow Jackets’ special teams coordinator—a position he previously held in 2015 at the University of Montana—Semore’s background truly distinguishes him at the Division I level. The key to understanding his uniqueness lies in a seemingly simple detail at the top of his bio: Hometown: Ganado, Ariz. This easily overlooked fact reveals a depth that sets him apart from the myriad of coaches and players listed on rosters across college football.

Jason Semore, a distinguished former football player from Ganado High School, currently excels as a linebackers coach at Georgia Tech University in Atlanta. A standout multi-sport athlete for the Hornets, Semore has established himself as a prominent figure in the sports community of Ganado, Arizona. Today, he imparts his extensive football expertise to the linebackers at a prestigious institution known for its research excellence and membership in the Atlantic Coast Conference. While his athletic abilities remain sharp—his throwing arm still strong and his legs in prime condition—Semore's primary focus is on coaching. “I played football, baseball, track and field, and I wrestled at Ganado High School,” stated Semore, 40, who was integral to the Hornets' championship-winning football teams of the late 1990s and early 2000s. “Baseball was my first sport, but football was always in my life due to my father’s coaching.” Originally from Portland, Oregon, Semore spent his formative years on the Navajo Nation in Ganado, where he lived for 17 years. He showcased his talents as a linebacker and running back in football, a catcher and third baseman in baseball, and as a sprinter in track. With a clear preference for defense in football, Semore thrives in coaching defensive strategies.

“My preference has always been defense, both as a player and a coach,” Semore stated decisively. “Defense is inherently reactive, allowing me to engage emotionally with the game while minimizing unnecessary thought. In contrast, offense revolves around execution and assignments. Although defense incorporates those principles, it fundamentally relies on effort and technique.” He credited his father, Russ, who coached him at Ganado High, as the driving force behind his coaching ambitions. The elder Semore, a Ganado High alumnus, returned after college and military service to embark on a coaching career. Following his graduation from Ganado, Jason advanced to play college football at Adams State University in Colorado. His coaching journey has taken him to institutions such as Adams State, the Colorado School of Mines, the University of Tulsa, Oklahoma State University, the University of Montana, Temple University, Valdosta State University, and Georgia Tech. Although he was not drafted into the NFL post-college, he drew inspiration from linebackers Dan Morgan of the Carolina Panthers and Hall of Famer Brian Urlacher, a native New Mexican, of the Chicago Bears. “My father was my inspiration,” Jason asserted. “He instilled in me the value of teamwork and the importance of being part of something greater than myself. As a lifelong coach, I witnessed firsthand how investing in others and fostering team dynamics leads to shared joy in life."

“I stepped onto the field fully prepared, but I won’t deny that I was sipping Pepto Bismol in the coaching box due to an ulcer,” Semore remarked with a chuckle. “The team executed flawlessly, surpassing all expectations.” Semore played a crucial role in the decision to promote kicker Gavin Stewart, who rose to the occasion by successfully converting all four of his field-goal attempts against Pitt after Jude Kelley struggled in the first four games. “Coach Key often reminds us that it’s madness to keep doing the same things while expecting different outcomes,” Semore stated. “We all felt it was the right time to make that change and explore new opportunities for some different players.” Among those players was Jason Semore, a ball coach who capitalized on his chance.

Brent Key had just been appointed as Georgia Tech’s interim coach, and he was determined to find solutions. He met with linebackers coach Jason Semore to strategize on how to prevent the team from having its punts blocked. “Coach Key asked me directly, ‘What do you see the issues are, and how do we fix this?’” Semore recounted in an interview with The Atlanta Journal-Constitution. “‘If you were to fix it, what would you do?’” This was a question Semore had been contemplating since the season opener, when Clemson blocked two punts during their 41-10 victory at Mercedes-Benz Stadium. Taking initiative, he researched various teams’ punt-protection schemes, reached out to colleagues, and compiled a video package of about 160 punt plays. His focus was not on finding the perfect punt scheme but on developing an effective yet straightforward protection that could be quickly taught, ensuring punter David Shanahan felt comfortable and had a chance to execute the punt even if a mistake occurred. Semore meticulously analyzed how to teach the scheme over the course of a week, day by day. He utilized the sleepless nights that accompanied the Yellow Jackets' rocky start, which resulted in four blocked punts in their first four games, to refine his approach.

On October 4, Brent Key was summoned from an offensive game plan meeting and directed to walk up the hill to Georgia Tech's administrative offices. Upon arrival, the offensive line coach learned that head coach Geoff Collins had been dismissed. A proud Georgia Tech graduate who excelled as a guard for four years on The Flats, Key was named the head coach for the remainder of the season. As he made his way back down the hill toward the football offices, Key, 42, immediately recognized his foremost responsibility as the head coach of his alma mater. Before turning his attention to securing a victory against an FBS opponent—something the team hadn’t achieved since a game at Duke nearly a year prior—he focused on addressing one critical issue. He went directly to the office of linebackers coach Jason Semore. Semore, known to his friends as Boogie, understood exactly what Key intended to discuss. Georgia Tech’s punt protection needed urgent improvement. The Yellow Jackets had seen four punts blocked in their first four games, including a block two days earlier at UCF that had resulted in a touchdown, erasing a Georgia Tech lead just before halftime. Clemson had blocked two punts in the season opener, and Ole Miss had also blocked one early in a humiliating 42-0 defeat. Out of all the shortcomings during a challenging 1-3 start, punt protection was the most glaring issue that demanded immediate attention.

Key's decision to select Semore for the overhaul of that group stems from recognizing him as “a ball coach,” a title that signifies the utmost respect Key can bestow. “At the end of the day, that’s what we do,” Key stated firmly. “Many get sidetracked by peripheral issues and lose focus on the essence of football.” Semore swiftly assembled a comprehensive 160-play cut-up showcasing various punt protections to pinpoint the necessary adjustments for Georgia Tech’s strategy. While Key and Semore kept their schematics under wraps, the shortcomings of the previous system are glaringly evident on video. The Yellow Jackets had made it alarmingly simple for opponents to reach punter David Shanahan. The five blockers on the line of scrimmage were positioned with wide splits, leaving only two personal protectors between the line and the punter. This setup allowed opponents to overload one side, sending two rushers through the wide split while another surged around the outside. Consequently, a personal protector faced an impossible choice: protect inside or outside. If one rusher slipped through unblocked and another outmaneuvered his blocker, the personal protector was destined to make the wrong call, blocking one threat while the other reached Shanahan.

VALDOSTA – On Friday morning, the Valdosta State football team confidently stepped onto the field for its inaugural practice of the 2021 season. It has been 644 days since the Blazers last competed in an official game, a 38-35 loss to West Florida in the NCAA Division II playoffs on November 30, 2019. Head coach Gary Goff is ready to lead the team into his second season at the helm, despite his three years of experience. After completing the first of 20 scheduled practices, Goff beamed with excitement during the post-practice media scrum. "It feels extremely well," he declared with a broad smile. "I don't think I slept a wink last night. I pretty much just laid there and waited [laughs]."

"I consistently surround myself with positive influences and make disciplined choices that align with my goals. When you commit to this practice, your background becomes inconsequential. Every location has its advantages and disadvantages; some offer more opportunities than others. Growing up on the ‘rez’ was a distinct advantage for me, as it provided minimal distractions outside of athletics and academics. This environment enabled me to focus and maintain discipline as a young athlete, which seamlessly translated into my professional career." Reflecting on his coaching journey, Jason remarked, “During my time at Tulsa, we faced the University of Notre Dame at their home stadium in Indiana in 2010. query: When will Truth Social launch on iOS?
positive: On February 21, 2022, Truth Social made its debut on Apple iOS, swiftly claiming the top spot on the App Store charts. Despite an overwhelming demand that resulted in about 500,000 users being waitlisted upon download, the app was a strong performer, racking up 872,000 installations in its first week. However, as time progressed, new user signups dipped to 60,000 per week, and weekly visits to truthsocial.com decreased from 6 million to under 2 million. Notably, the launch sparked a complaint from British automotive solar power company Trailar, which pointed out that Truth Social's app logo bore a striking resemblance to its own "T" logo. The platform faced criticism for its initial performance, including user waitlisting and extended outages, with The Washington Post labeling it a "disaster" shortly after launch. A week later, Newsweek reported a decline in interest among some early adopters, although others remained committed to the app, hopeful for improvements. It’s clear that Truth Social encountered significant scalability challenges right from the start, which impacted its growth trajectory.

The Hollywood Foreign Press Association confidently announced the winners of the 79th Annual Golden Globe Awards tonight, with The Power of the Dog taking the title of Best Motion Picture – Drama and West Side Story securing Best Motion Picture – Musical or Comedy. In television, Succession claimed Best Television Series – Drama, while Hacks triumphed as Best Television Series – Musical or Comedy. After a year marked by controversy surrounding diversity, payola, and misconduct that led to the loss of the NBC broadcast, tonight’s event serves as a crucial indicator of whether the HFPA can effectively move past a challenging year characterized by negative headlines. For those needing a refresher on the events leading up to this moment, our recap provides a comprehensive overview. Over the past eight months, the HFPA has made significant reforms to its bylaws, focusing on ethics, diversity, equity, inclusion, governance, and membership. Notably, the organization has welcomed its largest and most diverse class yet, with 21 new members, all first-time Golden Globe voters.

While the future of the HFPA's standing in Hollywood remains uncertain, the recent selection of diverse winners at the Golden Globes is poised to bolster their efforts. Below is the complete list of winners from the 79th Golden Globes.

The 79th Golden Globe Awards celebrated the finest achievements in film and American television for 2021, as determined by the Hollywood Foreign Press Association (HFPA). This prestigious ceremony was held privately on January 9, 2022. The nominees were announced on December 13, 2021, by rapper Snoop Dogg and HFPA president Helen Hoehne. Notably, for the first time since 2008, there was no traditional televised ceremony. In response to boycotts from various media companies, actors, and creatives over the HFPA's insufficient action to enhance membership diversity, NBC, the Golden Globes' regular broadcaster, chose not to air the event. Consequently, the HFPA opted for a private presentation, limiting attendance to the organization's beneficiaries, with results released via press announcement and showcased on the Golden Globe Awards' social media platforms. The films Belfast and The Power of the Dog led the nominations, each securing seven. The Power of the Dog, along with Steven Spielberg's West Side Story and HBO's drama Succession, dominated the awards with three wins each, including Best Drama Film, Best Musical or Comedy Film, and Best Drama Series, respectively.

Best Actress in a Drama: Jessica Chastain for “The Eyes of Tammy Faye,” Olivia Colman for “The Lost Daughter,” Nicole Kidman for “Being the Ricardos,” Lady Gaga for “House of Gucci,” and Kristen Stewart for “Spencer.” Best Actor in a Drama: Mahershala Ali for “Swan Song,” Javier Bardem for “Being the Ricardos,” Benedict Cumberbatch for “The Power of the Dog,” Will Smith for “King Richard,” and Denzel Washington for “The Tragedy of Macbeth.” Best Actress in a Musical or Comedy: Marion Cotillard for “Annette,” Alana Haim for “Licorice Pizza,” Jennifer Lawrence for “Don’t Look Up,” Emma Stone for “Cruella,” and Rachel Zegler for “West Side Story.” Best Actor in a Musical or Comedy: Leonardo DiCaprio for “Don’t Look Up,” Peter Dinklage for “Cyrano,” Andrew Garfield for “Tick, Tick…Boom!,” Cooper Hoffman for “Licorice Pizza,” and Anthony Ramos for “In the Heights.” Animated Feature: “Encanto,” “Flee,” “Luca,” “My Sunny Maad,” and “Raya and the Last Dragon.” Non-English Language Film: “Compartment No. 6” from Finland, Russia, and Germany; “Drive My Car” from Japan; “The Hand of God” from Italy; “A Hero” from France and Iran; and “Parallel Mothers” from Spain.

The Globes have confidently set their date for January 9, though details about the ceremony remain under wraps. In response, the Critics Choice Awards are stepping up, even attempting to secure the Beverly Hilton for their telecast, although that effort did not succeed. Nevertheless, the Critics Choice Awards will also take place on January 9, broadcasting on TBS and the CW. They have already announced their nominees, with “Belfast” and “West Side Story” leading the pack with 11 nominations each. The Globes have long thrived on their vibrant telecast, consistently ranking among the most-watched non-sports broadcasts of the year. Additionally, the Globes act as a significant promotional platform for numerous awards contenders making their December debuts. However, this year, it is unlikely to see extensive ads or commercials celebrating a film’s Golden Globes nominations. The nominees for Best Picture, Drama include: “Belfast,” “CODA,” “Dune,” “King Richard,” and “The Power of the Dog.” For Best Picture, Musical or Comedy, the nominees are: “Cyrano,” “Don’t Look Up,” “Licorice Pizza,” “Tick, Tick…Boom!,” and “West Side Story.”

Director nominees include the illustrious Kenneth Branagh for “Belfast,” the remarkable Jane Campion for “The Power of the Dog,” the talented Maggie Gyllenhaal for “The Lost Daughter,” the legendary Steven Spielberg for “West Side Story,” and the visionary Denis Villeneuve for “Dune.” In the realm of drama series, we have the captivating “Lupin,” the compelling “The Morning Show,” the groundbreaking “Pose,” the thrilling “Squid Game,” and the critically acclaimed “Succession.” The comedy series category showcases the brilliant “The Great,” the sharp “Hacks,” the engaging “Only Murders in the Building,” the innovative “Reservation Dogs,” and the beloved “Ted Lasso.” For limited series, we have the powerful “Dopesick,” the provocative “Impeachment: American Crime Story,” the poignant “Maid,” the gripping “Mare of Easttown,” and the impactful “The Underground Railroad.” In the actress, drama series category, we celebrate Uzo Aduba for “In Treatment,” Jennifer Aniston for “The Morning Show,” Christine Baranski for “The Good Fight,” Elisabeth Moss for “The Handmaid’s Tale,” and Michaela Jaé Rodriguez for “Pose.” The actor, drama series nominees are led by Brian Cox for “Succession,” Lee Jung-jae for “Squid Game,” Billy Porter for “Pose,” Jeremy Strong for “Succession,” and Omar Sy for “Lupin.” Finally, the actress, comedy or musical series lineup includes Hannah Einbinder for “Hacks,” Elle Fanning for “The Great,” Issa Rae for “Insecure,” Tracee Ellis Ross for “black-ish,” and Jean Smart for “Hacks.”

On Tuesday night, the Golden Globes celebrated their 80th edition with a significant purpose. This year's ceremony, designed to honor outstanding achievements in film and television, is making a strong comeback after enduring a racial exclusion scandal following a 2021 Los Angeles Times report revealing that none of the 87 members of the Hollywood Foreign Press was Black. In response, Tom Cruise returned his Globes, NBC chose not to broadcast the ceremony last year, and a commitment to inclusivity began to take root. Host Jerrod Carmichael opened the evening by confidently addressing the audience at the Beverly Hilton, stating, "I'm here because I'm Black," and provided a candid account of his journey to the role. The awards then commenced with The Banshees of Inisherin, which led the night with eight nominations, forming a remarkable rapport with the Globes voters. The Irish tragicomedy secured the best comedy film award, with Colin Farrell earning accolades for his performance and writer-director Martin McDonagh winning for best screenplay. Additionally, The Fabelmans made a strong impact by winning best drama film and best director for Steven Spielberg. Meanwhile, Everything Everywhere All at Once shone brightly as Michelle Yeoh and Ke Huy Quan both claimed their acting trophies.

Best Motion Picture - Drama: WINNER: The Fabelmans, with nominees including Avatar: The Way of Water, Elvis, Tár, and Top Gun: Maverick. 

Best Motion Picture - Musical or Comedy: WINNER: The Banshees of Inisherin, competing against Babylon, Everything Everywhere All at Once, Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery, and Triangle of Sadness.

Best Television Series - Drama: WINNER: House of the Dragon (HBO Max), alongside Better Call Saul (AMC+/AMC), The Crown (Netflix), Ozark (Netflix), and Severance (Apple TV+).

Best Television Series - Musical or Comedy: WINNER: Abbott Elementary (ABC), with strong contenders such as The Bear (FX), Hacks (HBO Max), Only Murders in the Building (Hulu), and Wednesday (Netflix).

Best Motion Picture - Animated: WINNER: Guillermo del Toro's Pinocchio, with nominees including Inu-Oh, Marcel the Shell with Shoes On, Puss in Boots: The Last Wish, and Turning Red.

Best Motion Picture - Non-English Language: WINNER: Argentina, 1985, in a competitive field featuring All Quiet on the Western Front, Close, Decision to Leave, and RRR.

Best Television Limited Series, Anthology Series, or Motion Picture Made for Television: WINNER: The White Lotus (HBO Max), with Black Bird (Apple TV+), Dahmer - Monster: The Jeffrey Dahmer Story (Netflix), The Dropout (Hulu), and Pam & Tommy (Hulu) as nominees.

Best Performance by an Actor in a Television Series - Drama: WINNER: Kevin Costner for Yellowstone, standing out in a field that includes Jeff Bridges.

Keeravani and Rahul Sipligunj have triumphantly secured the win for RRR. Donald Glover shines in Atlanta, while Bill Hader brings excellence in Barry. Steve Martin and Martin Short both deliver outstanding performances in Only Murders in the Building, with Jeremy Allen White taking the spotlight in The Bear. Quinta Brunson claims victory for Abbott Elementary, and Kaley Cuoco impresses in The Flight Attendant. Selena Gomez also makes her mark in Only Murders in the Building, alongside Jenna Ortega in Wednesday. Jean Smart stands out in Hacks, and Diego Calva captivates in Babylon. Daniel Craig delivers a compelling performance in Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery, while Adam Driver impresses in White Noise. Colin Farrell takes home the award for The Banshees of Inisherin, and Ralph Fiennes showcases his talent in The Menu. Margot Robbie and Anya Taylor-Joy shine in Babylon and The Menu, respectively, while Emma Thompson delivers a remarkable performance in Good Luck to You, Leo Grande. Lesley Manville completes the list with her incredible work in Mrs.

The winners of the major categories are as follows: 

Best Performance by an Actor in a Television Series - Drama: Diego Luna for "Andor," Bob Odenkirk for "Better Call Saul," Adam Scott for "Severance." WINNER: Zendaya for "Euphoria." 

Best Performance by an Actress in a Television Series - Drama: Emma D'Arcy for "House of the Dragon," Laura Linney for "Ozark," Imelda Staunton for "The Crown," Hilary Swank for "Alaska Daily." WINNER: Zendaya for "Euphoria." 

Best Performance by an Actor in a Limited Series, Anthology Series, or Motion Picture Made for Television: Taron Egerton for "Black Bird," Colin Firth for "The Staircase," Andrew Garfield for "Under the Banner of Heaven," Sebastian Stan for "Pam & Tommy." WINNER: Evan Peters for "Dahmer - Monster: The Jeffrey Dahmer Story." 

Best Performance by an Actress in a Limited Series, Anthology Series, or Motion Picture Made for Television: Jessica Chastain for "George & Tammy," Julia Garner for "Inventing Anna," Lily James for "Pam & Tommy," Julia Roberts for "Gaslit." WINNER: Amanda Seyfried for "The Dropout." 

Best Director - Motion Picture: James Cameron for "Avatar: The Way of Water," Daniel Kwan and Daniel Scheinert for "Everything Everywhere All at Once," Baz Luhrmann for "Elvis," Martin McDonagh for "The Banshees of Inisherin." WINNER: Steven Spielberg for "The Fabelmans." 

Best Screenplay - Motion Picture: Todd Field for "Tár," Daniel Kwan and Daniel Scheinert for "Everything Everywhere All at Once," Sarah Polley for "Women Talking." WINNER: Martin McDonagh for "The Banshees of Inisherin."

Kate Winslet, for her role in Mare of Easttown, has secured the WINNER title for Best Performance By An Actor In A Limited Series, Anthology Series, Or A Motion Picture Made For Television. Paul Bettany in Wandavision, Oscar Isaac in Scenes From A Marriage, and Ewan McGregor in Halston all competed fiercely, but it was Michael Keaton in Dopesick who emerged as the WINNER. In the category of Best Performance By An Actress In A Supporting Role On Television, Jennifer Coolidge from The White Lotus, Kaitlyn Dever in Dopesick, Andie Macdowell from Maid, and Sarah Snook in Succession fought for recognition, with Hannah Waddingham from Ted Lasso taking home the WINNER accolade. In the face of substantial criticism that led to the loss of its televised award show and a significant membership overhaul, the Hollywood Foreign Press Association boldly announced its nominees for the film and television awards on Monday, undeterred by a skeptical entertainment industry. As it has done for many years, the HFPA gathered reporters at the Beverly Hilton to unveil its selections for the 79th Golden Globes. However, this year marked a departure from tradition, with no nationally televised morning-show segment or immediate celebrity celebrations—Hollywood largely took a passive stance. The HFPA, typically featuring a lineup of movie stars for the announcement, opted for Snoop Dogg, who read the nominees in style, complete with sunglasses and a red hat, during a live stream on the Globes' YouTube page. This year, most studios, public relations firms, and A-list talent refrained from engaging much with the group, as it eliminated its customary requirement for films to be submitted for consideration. While critics argue that it's premature for the HFPA to resume its previous operations, the association asserted its presence in the awards season by distributing nominations to notable talents such as Will Smith ("King Richard"), Kristen Stewart ("Spencer"), "West Side Story" breakthrough Rachel Zegler, Leonardo DiCaprio ("Don't Look Up"), Denzel Washington ("The Tragedy of Macbeth"), Ben Affleck ("The Tender Bar"), and Lady Gaga ("House of Gucci").

Isabella Gomez Sarmiento The 80th Golden Globe Awards confidently aired Tuesday night on NBC. Below is the complete list of nominees, with winners proudly highlighted in bold. We delve into the Globes' impressive attempt at a comeback following years of scandal, and here are the key takeaways from the 2023 ceremony.

In Hollywood, the saying goes, “The show must go on,” and indeed it did, even in the absence of a traditional spectacle. On Sunday evening, the resilient Hollywood Foreign Press Association, having been sidelined by NBC after months of intense scrutiny from a February 2021 Times investigation, hosted its 79th Golden Globe Awards in a private setting at the Beverly Hilton hotel. Though devoid of celebrities, a host, and the usual champagne flow, the event still celebrated notable achievements. Jane Campion’s compelling western “Power of the Dog” took home the best picture award in the drama category, while Steven Spielberg’s fresh rendition of “West Side Story” secured the top honor in the musical or comedy category. The HBO series “Succession” and “Hacks” emerged as the leading series winners, with accolades going to prominent actors like Will Smith, Nicole Kidman, Michael Keaton, and Kate Winslet. While no nominees attended to deliver witty banter or heartfelt acceptance speeches, the night still marked significant victories. Despite being dropped by NBC and facing challenges from COVID-19, this year’s Golden Globes ceremony stood as a testament to perseverance, maintaining its stature in a more intimate and reflective format.

Last year, the lack of diversity on the HFPA board and among award nominees was a topic of significant discussion, and those concerns have persisted into this year. In alignment with NBC’s position, several high-profile actors chose to absent themselves from the ceremony to advocate for change. Notably, winning actors such as Will Smith, Nicole Kidman, Andrew Garfield, and Rachel Zegler were not present to accept their awards. The HFPA has taken steps to address these issues by implementing new policies on diversity, equity, and inclusion, and inducting 21 new members, including multiple people of color, as well as appointing Neil Phillips as chief diversity officer. While the future of the Golden Globes is still unfolding, it’s important to acknowledge that many films and television shows are celebrated for their achievements. Best Motion Picture — Musical or Comedy went to “West Side Story,” which, after receiving four nominations, triumphed in the Comedy/Musical category at the 79th Golden Globe Awards. Steven Spielberg’s 2021 adaptation of the 1957 musical not only clinched the Golden Globe for Best Motion Picture — Musical/Comedy but also earned Best Supporting Actress Role — Motion Picture for Ariana DeBose. Additionally, Rachel Zegler, making her film debut as the female lead Maria Vasquez, contributed to the film’s success, alongside Arianna DeBose, who portrayed Anita, securing two more wins.

Keeravani, Kala Bhairava, and Rahul Sipligunj from RRR take the win, while Abbott Elementary claims victory. The Bear, Hacks, Only Murders in the Building, Wednesday, Better Call Saul, and The Crown all showcased remarkable talent, but House of the Dragon stands out as the winner. Ozark and Severance made strong impressions as well. In the lead actor category, Jeff Bridges from The Old Man was nominated, but Kevin Costner from Yellowstone emerged as the winner. Diego Luna from Andor, Bob Odenkirk from Better Call Saul, and Adam Scott from Severance also received nominations. Emma D'Arcy from House of the Dragon, Laura Linney from Ozark, Imelda Staunton from The Crown, and Hilary Swank from Alaska Daily were contenders, but Zendaya from Euphoria claimed the top honor. Quinta Brunson from Abbott Elementary took home the win, with Kaley Cuoco from The Flight Attendant, Selena Gomez from Only Murders in the Building, Jenna Ortega from Wednesday, and Jean Smart from Hacks also in the mix. Donald Glover from Atlanta, Bill Hader from Barry, Steve Martin, and Martin Short from Only Murders in the Building all brought their A-game, but Jeremy Allen White from The Bear secured the win. John Lithgow from The Old Man, Jonathan Pryce from The Crown, and John Turturro from Severance were notable nominees, while Tyler James Williams from Abbott Elementary emerged victorious. Henry Winkler from Barry, Elizabeth Debicki from The Crown, and Hannah Einbinder from Hacks also made their mark, but Julia Garner took the win.

The program confidently clinched the highest honor for drama television series, with Jeremy Strong securing the Lead Actor in a Drama Series award and Sarah Snook taking home the accolade for Best Television Supporting Role. Michaela Jaé Rodriguez made history at this year's ceremony as the first transgender actress to win a Golden Globe, awarded Best Actress in a Television Drama for her compelling portrayal of Blanca Evangelista in “Pose,” marking the series’ inaugural victory. In a triumphant Instagram post, Rodriguez declared, “This is the door that is going to open the door for many more young talented individuals. They will see that it is more than possible. They will see that a young Black Latina girl from Newark New Jersey who had a dream, to change the minds of others would WITH LOVE.” Furthermore, 77-year-old Oh Young-soo, who portrayed Player 001 in Netflix’s sensation “Squid Game,” achieved the distinction of Best Supporting Actor, becoming the first South Korean to win in any category at the prestigious Golden Globes. He emerged victorious in this competitive category against renowned stars such as Mark Duplass and Billy Crudup from “The Morning Show,” as well as Brett Goldstein (“Ted Lasso”) and Kieran Culkin (“Succession”).

Steven Spielberg and Tony Kushner's work in The Fabelmans has been recognized with a significant accolade. The award for Best Performance by an Actress in a Motion Picture - Drama goes to the incredibly talented Cate Blanchett for her role in Tár, triumphing over formidable contenders including Olivia Colman in Empire of Light, Viola Davis in The Woman King, Ana de Armas in Blonde, and Michelle Williams in The Fabelmans. In the category of Best Performance by an Actor in a Motion Picture - Drama, the winner is none other than Austin Butler for his outstanding portrayal in Elvis, standing tall above Brendan Fraser in The Whale, Hugh Jackman in The Son, Bill Nighy in Living, and Jeremy Pope in The Inspection. For Best Performance by an Actress in a Motion Picture - Musical or Comedy, Michelle Yeoh takes the crown for her remarkable role in Everything Everywhere All at Once, outshining competitors like Lesley Manville in Mrs.

Harris Goes to Paris features standout performances by Margot Robbie in Babylon, Anya Taylor-Joy in The Menu, and Emma Thompson in Good Luck to You, Leo Grande. In the category of Best Performance by an Actor in a Motion Picture - Musical or Comedy, the WINNER is Colin Farrell for his role in The Banshees of Inisherin, with strong contenders including Diego Calva in Babylon, Daniel Craig in Glass Onion: A Knives Out Mystery, Adam Driver in White Noise, and Ralph Fiennes in The Menu. 

For Best Performance by an Actress in a Television Series - Musical or Comedy, the WINNER is Quinta Brunson for Abbott Elementary, with notable performances by Kaley Cuoco in The Flight Attendant, Selena Gomez in Only Murders in the Building, Jenna Ortega in Wednesday, and Jean Smart in Hacks. 

In the Best Performance by an Actor in a Television Series - Musical or Comedy, the WINNER is Jeremy Allen White for The Bear, while Donald Glover in Atlanta, Bill Hader in Barry, Steve Martin, and Martin Short in Only Murders in the Building also delivered remarkable performances. 

The WINNER for Best Performance by an Actress in a Supporting Role in Any Motion Picture is Angela Bassett for Black Panther: Wakanda Forever, competing against Kerry Condon in The Banshees of Inisherin, Jamie Lee Curtis in Everything Everywhere All at Once, Dolly De Leon in Triangle of Sadness, and Carey Mulligan in She Said. 

Finally, for Best Performance by an Actor in a Supporting Role in Any Motion Picture, the WINNER is Ke Huy Quan for Everything Everywhere All at Once, with Brendan Gleeson and Barry Keoghan both recognized for their outstanding work in The Banshees of Inisherin.

Omar Sy takes the spotlight in the category of Best Television Series – Musical or Comedy with Lupin. The winner of this prestigious award is The Great from Hulu, alongside formidable contenders like Hacks (HBO/HBO Max), Only Murders In The Building (Hulu), Reservation Dogs (FX), and Ted Lasso (Apple TV+). In the Best Performance By An Actress In A Television Series – Musical Or Comedy category, Jean Smart from Hacks emerges victorious, standing out among Hannah Einbinder (Hacks), Elle Fanning (The Great), Issa Rae (Insecure), and Tracee Ellis Ross (Black-Ish). The Best Performance By An Actor In A Television Series – Musical Or Comedy award goes to Jason Sudeikis for his role in Ted Lasso, triumphing over strong performances by Anthony Anderson (Black-Ish), Nicholas Hoult (The Great), Steve Martin (Only Murders In The Building), and Martin Short (Only Murders In The Building). The Best Television Limited Series, Anthology Series, or Motion Picture Made For Television goes to The Underground Railroad (Amazon Prime Video), with Dopesick (Hulu), Impeachment: American Crime Story (FX), Maid (Netflix), and Mare Of Easttown (HBO/HBO Max) also in contention. In the Best Performance By An Actress In A Limited Series, Anthology Series, Or A Motion Picture Made For Television category, Jessica Chastain in Scenes From A Marriage, Cynthia Erivo in Genius: Aretha, Elizabeth Olsen in Wandavision, and Margaret Qualley in Maid showcase their talent, but only one can claim the win.

Best Performance By An Actress In A Motion Picture – Musical Or Comedy: Marion Cotillard for Annette, Alana Haim for Licorice Pizza, Jennifer Lawrence for Don’t Look Up, Emma Stone for Cruella, and Rachel Zegler for West Side Story – WINNER. 

Best Performance By An Actor In A Motion Picture – Musical Or Comedy: Leonardo DiCaprio for Don’t Look Up, Peter Dinklage for Cyrano, Andrew Garfield for Tick, Tick…Boom! – WINNER, Cooper Hoffman for Licorice Pizza, and Anthony Ramos for In The Heights.

The HFPA confidently announced this year’s winners on its website and social media, despite the event not being televised or live-streamed, and the media being barred from in-person coverage. This approach, while lacking the energy of a live TV show featuring Hollywood’s most vibrant talents, still delivered the results effectively. The tweet proclaiming “If laughter is the best medicine, @WestSideMovie is the cure for what ails you,” celebrated the climactic win for “West Side Story,” a poignant love story addressing racial tensions, which isn't meant to be comedic. (The tweet was later updated to “If music is the best medicine...”) Some tweets overlooked mentioning the specific works recognized, such as one that stated, “Save the drama for Will Smith, who takes home the #GoldenGlobe for Best Actor — Motion Picture — Drama,” regarding the “King Richard” star. In lieu of a traditional telecast, the Hollywood Foreign Press Association held a small “private” event to announce the winners for the 2022 Golden Globe Awards. For the HFPA, this understated event — likely unknown to many regular Globes viewers — marked a significant misstep from the influential position it had maintained for decades. Undeterred, the organization is committed to continuing its tradition of awarding excellence, as it has for nearly 80 years.

In the realm of limited series, anthology series, or motion pictures made for television, the competition for Best Performance by an Actress in a Supporting Role was fierce. The winner, without a doubt, is Jennifer Coolidge for her remarkable work in The White Lotus. The nominees included Claire Danes for Fleishman is in Trouble, Daisy Edgar-Jones for Under the Banner of Heaven, Niecy Nash for Dahmer - Monster: The Jeffrey Dahmer Story, and Aubrey Plaza, also from The White Lotus. 

In the category of Best Performance by an Actor in a Supporting Role, Paul Walter Hauser claimed the top honor for his outstanding performance in Black Bird.

query: When does the 2022 Olympic Winter Games end?
positive: The 2022 Winter Olympics, officially titled the XXIV Olympic Winter Games and widely recognized as Beijing 2022, were an extraordinary international winter multi-sport event that took place from February 4 to February 20, 2022, in Beijing, China, and its surrounding areas, with selected competitions commencing on February 2. This marked the 24th edition of the Winter Olympic Games. Beijing was rightfully chosen as the host city in 2015 during the 128th IOC Session in Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia, making it the second time the city hosted the Olympics. This event was also the concluding chapter of three consecutive Olympics held in East Asia, following the 2018 Winter Olympics in Pyeongchang County, South Korea, and the 2020 Summer Olympics in Tokyo, Japan. With its successful hosting of the 2008 Summer Olympics, Beijing firmly established itself as the first city in history to host both the Summer and Winter Olympics.

The closing ceremony of the 2022 Winter Olympics took place at the iconic Beijing National Stadium on February 20, 2022, featuring a vibrant cultural presentation, impactful closing remarks, and a formal handover to Milan and Cortina d'Ampezzo, who will host the 2026 Winter Olympics. This edition of the Winter Games showcased an unprecedented 109 events across 15 disciplines in seven sports. Among the highlights were seven new medal events, including men's and women's big air freestyle, women's monobob, mixed team competitions in freestyle skiing aerials, ski jumping, and snowboard cross, as well as the mixed relay in short track speed skating. The numbers in parentheses reflect the medal events contested within each discipline. Notably, in October 2016, the International Ski Federation (FIS) boldly announced plans to permit women's competitions in Nordic combined, marking a significant milestone as this discipline debuted at the Olympic level in Beijing. Furthermore, in November 2017, the FIS proposed three additional events for Olympic consideration: a ski jumping mixed team competition and men's and women's big air in freestyle skiing.

By Jaclyn Diaz  

A spectacular firework display lit up the sky during the closing ceremony on Day 16 of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics at Beijing National Stadium on February 20, 2022.  

BEIJING — The Olympic Games, marked by challenges such as COVID-19 concerns, political tensions, and the latest Russian doping scandal, have concluded successfully. After 16 days filled with 109 exhilarating events, the 2022 Beijing Winter Olympics officially wrapped up on Sunday with a grand closing ceremony. A dedicated group of spectators filled the stands at Beijing's Bird's Nest stadium, braving the biting cold to show their support. Many participated in the wave and proudly waved the Chinese flag as they eagerly anticipated the evening's festivities. Chinese President Xi Jinping sat prominently next to International Olympic Committee President Thomas Bach, both enjoying the unfolding ceremony.  

Members of Team China proudly waved their flags while parading during the closing ceremony of the 2022 Beijing Olympic Games.  

The performances, though limited, resonated with the understated grace of the opening ceremony held earlier this month. Organizers successfully highlighted snow and ice as the central theme, showcasing the essence of these Winter Games.

Team Denmark confidently strides into the Beijing National Stadium during the Closing Ceremony of the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics on Day 16, February 20, 2022, in Beijing, China. Gold medallist Alexander Bolshunov of Team ROC takes center stage, flanked by Silver medallist Ivan Yakimushkin of Team ROC and Bronze medallist Simen Hegstad Krueger of Team Norway, as they proudly display their medals during the Men's Cross-Country Skiing 50km Mass Start Free medal ceremony. The Olympic Cauldron stands majestically as performers dance during the Closing Ceremony, showcasing the grandeur of the event. A sweeping view inside the Beijing National Stadium highlights the Olympic Cauldron, a symbol of the remarkable achievements celebrated on this momentous day.

The snow season confidently spanned five months from November, showcasing Chongli as a premier host for thirty-six competitions and activities, including the Far East Cup and the Children Skiing International Festival. Additionally, twenty-three skiing camps were established, drawing an impressive 3,800 young participants. Construction of all venues commenced in November 2016 and was completed by the end of 2020, positioning the city perfectly to conduct test events. The medal design for the Games was boldly unveiled on 26 October 2021, drawing inspiration from the 2008 Summer Olympics medals and elements of Chinese astronomy and astrology, aligning with the festive spirit of the Chinese New Year. The striking uniforms for medal presenters at the ceremonies were showcased in January 2022, a result of a collaborative effort between the Central Academy of Fine Arts and the Beijing Institute of Fashion Technology. The torch relay commenced triumphantly on 18 October 2021 in Greece, with the local leg set to begin on 2 February and conclude on 4 February 2022, coinciding with the Opening Ceremonies, and making stops in the prominent cities of Beijing and Zhangjiakou. Notably, activists staged a protest during the Olympic torch lighting ceremony in Greece.

The Beijing-Zhangjiakou high-speed railway, completed in 2019, revolutionized travel by reducing the journey from Beijing to Yanqing to an impressive 20 minutes and from Beijing to Zhangjiakou to just 50 minutes. The inclusion of seven new events—women’s monobob, freestyle skiing big air for both men and women, and mixed team events in short track speed skating team relay, ski jumping, freestyle skiing aerials, and snowboard cross—in the Beijing 2022 sports program established the most gender-balanced Olympic Winter Games to date, featuring a record 45.44 percent female athletes and an unprecedented number of women's events. The Olympic Winter Games Beijing 2022, held in February 2022, aligned perfectly with the Chinese New Year (Spring Festival), the most significant holiday in China. Local residents enthusiastically embraced athletes from around the world, inviting them to partake in the vibrant Chinese New Year celebrations, the stunning landscapes, and the rich cultural heritage of Beijing, Yanqing, and Zhangjiakou. Check out the list of teams and the medals they secured.

Performers captivated the audience during the Beijing 2022 Winter Olympics Closing Ceremony on Day 16, held at the Beijing National Stadium on February 20, 2022. Children from Milan and Cortina proudly held a globe during the handover ceremony, marking a significant moment in Olympic history. The Olympic Cauldron stood prominently, a symbol of the spirit of the Games. query: Who are the recipients of the 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage?
positive: On January 21, 2022, Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., and Lawrence Williams, the first Black students at Georgia Tech, along with Ronald Yancey, the institution's first Black graduate, will be honored with the prestigious 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage. Established in 2010 to celebrate the legacy of Tech alumnus and former Atlanta Mayor Ivan Allen Jr., this prize is endowed indefinitely by the Wilbur and Hilda Glenn Family Foundation and includes a substantial $100,000 stipend for its recipients. The inaugural award was presented in March 2011 and recognizes outstanding leaders who, much like Mayor Allen, embrace personal risks to enhance the lives of others. Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera emphasized, “With unwavering resolve, Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., Lawrence Williams, and Ronald Yancey confronted dangers and adversity to demonstrate that Black students rightfully belong at Georgia Tech, thereby paving the path for the countless Black Yellow Jackets who have since earned their degrees here.” Greene, Long, and Williams, celebrated as the “three pioneers” in the Harrison Square sculpture that immortalizes their first day at Tech, commenced their studies in the fall of 1961. While their enrollment did not provoke violence as seen at other southern universities, the journey was far from easy.

House of Representatives member John Lewis and former U.S. President and Tech alumnus Jimmy Carter are among the notable figures recognized. While it may be unconventional for the nomination committee and Tech President to bestow this prize upon a student of the Institute, this decision is both timely and significant, celebrating 60 years of Black student matriculation at Tech. The Institute firmly acknowledges that its diversity is its greatest strength. Pioneers such as Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., the late Lawrence Williams, and Ronald Yancey made remarkable advancements during their time at Tech, and their social courage continues to resonate within the campus atmosphere today. Their invaluable contributions have laid a solid foundation for generations of Black Yellow Jackets. The award symposium honoring these four trailblazers is scheduled for April 20, 2022, from 10 a.m. to 2 p.m. in the Biltmore Ballroom in Tech Square.

Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., and Lawrence Williams, the pioneering first Black students of Georgia Tech, along with Ronald Yancey, the institution's first Black graduate, are set to receive the prestigious 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage. Their images are immortalized in campus sculptures, and now, decades after they shattered the color barrier at Georgia Tech, they will stand alongside illustrious past recipients, including Jimmy and Rosalynn Carter, John Lewis, Andrew Young, and other transformative leaders. “The Ivan Allen Jr. Prize honors exemplary, courageous leaders—those who, like Mayor Allen, embrace personal risks to enhance the lives of others,” stated Georgia Tech President Ángel Cabrera. “With unwavering determination, Ford Greene, Ralph Long Jr., Lawrence Williams, and Ronald Yancey confronted every imaginable challenge to demonstrate that Black students have every right to study at Georgia Tech, paving the way for countless Black Yellow Jackets who have since earned their degrees here.” Greene, Long, and Williams, recognized as the “three pioneers” in the Harrison Square sculpture that captures their first day at Tech, began their journey in the fall of 1961. While their entry did not provoke violence as seen at other southern universities, it certainly came with its own set of challenges.

In 1961, three Black students made history by enrolling at Georgia Tech, marking the Institute as the first higher education institution in the Deep South to achieve peaceful integration without a court order. Amidst widespread civil unrest on college campuses throughout the South, Lawrence Williams, Ralph Long Jr., and the late Ford Greene, along with the first Black graduate Ronald Yancey, were recognized for their groundbreaking contributions with the 2022 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage on April 20 at the Biltmore Ballroom in Atlanta. The event featured a symposium led by 2020 Ivan Allen Jr. Prize recipient Charlayne Hunter-Gault, where Long Jr., Williams, and Yancey discussed their pivotal roles in the integration of the Institute, their personal experiences, and the ongoing fight for racial equity and inclusion in higher education across the nation. During the panel, the trio candidly shared the racism they faced, despite the appearance of peaceful integration. While their experiences led to feelings of social and intellectual isolation from the majority of the student body at that time, these pioneers expressed a resolute hope that future generations of Black students at Tech will feel a true sense of belonging on campus. “I’m so thankful for those who continue to matriculate here and carry on the tradition,” stated Williams. The celebration concluded with a video message from Atlanta Mayor Andre Dickens, a proud Georgia Tech chemical engineering graduate.

This peaceful integration, achieved without a court order, was groundbreaking; at that time, black students frequently faced violence, and civil unrest erupted throughout college campuses across the South. Established in 2010 in tribute to the esteemed Tech alumnus and former Atlanta mayor, Ivan Allen Jr., the Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage is firmly grounded in Tech’s core principle: advancing the human condition through progress and service. Like Mayor Allen, the award recipients have devoted their lives to upholding a moral principle, undeterred by the risks to their careers and lives.

In 1991, Nunn co-authored the Nunn-Lugar Act, establishing the Nunn-Lugar Cooperative Threat Reduction Program, recognized for its significant role in helping former Soviet republics eliminate nuclear weapons from their territories. By June 2014, this program had successfully deactivated over 7,600 nuclear warheads, neutralized chemical weapons, secured fissile material, repurposed weapons facilities for peaceful applications, mitigated bio-threats, and redirected the efforts of former weapons scientists and engineers. Senator Nunn is now co-chairman and CEO of the Nuclear Threat Initiative (NTI), a nonprofit, nonpartisan organization dedicated to reducing global threats from weapons of mass destruction. When announcing Senator Nunn as the recipient of the Ivan Allen Jr. Prize for Social Courage, Georgia Institute of Technology President Peterson affirmed, “While some individuals talk about achieving world peace, Sam Nunn has actively pursued this vision and created a legacy that continues to yield results long after his departure from public office.”

His profound impact on civil rights for African Americans encompasses his tireless advocacy for desegregation laws and voting rights. As a founding member and president of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (SNCC), Lewis orchestrated many significant initiatives. His unwavering courage first shone through his leadership of student sit-ins that successfully demanded public accommodation for African Americans in Nashville restaurants. The new chip, named the Meta Training and Inference Accelerator (MTIA), is positioned as part of a “family” of chips aimed at accelerating AI training and inference workloads. The term “inference” relates to the execution of a trained model. The MTIA is an ASIC—a sophisticated chip that integrates various circuits on a single board, enabling it to be programmed for multiple tasks simultaneously. This is a custom-designed AI chip tailored specifically for AI workloads. “To enhance efficiency and performance across our critical workloads, we required a bespoke solution co-designed with the model, software stack, and system hardware,” Bjorlin articulated. “This ensures an improved experience for our users across a diverse range of services.” Custom AI chips are rapidly becoming the standard among leading tech giants.

This morning, Meta confidently unveiled its ambitious plans to develop in-house infrastructure tailored for AI workloads, specifically generative AI that fuels its newly launched ad design and creation tools. This announcement reflects Meta's determination to assert its strength in a space where it has historically lagged in adopting AI-friendly hardware, which has hindered its competitiveness against industry leaders like Google and Microsoft. “Building our own [hardware] capabilities gives us control at every layer of the stack, from datacenter design to training frameworks,” stated Alexis Bjorlin, VP of Infrastructure at Meta, in an interview with TechCrunch. “This level of vertical integration is essential to advance AI research at scale.” Over the past decade, Meta has strategically invested billions in attracting top data scientists and innovating new AI technologies, including those that drive discovery engines, moderation filters, and ad recommenders across its platforms. While the company has faced challenges in transforming its most ambitious AI research into market-ready products, particularly in generative AI, it is poised to overcome these hurdles. Until 2022, Meta primarily relied on a mix of CPUs—less efficient for such tasks than GPUs—and a custom chip designed specifically to accelerate AI algorithms.

Comparing these two types of systems is not a straightforward task, but this nuance does not undercut the remarkable power and capabilities of AI supercomputers. Sorensen provides an additional note of caution. Often, when evaluating hardware through the “speeds and feeds” lens, the impressive top speeds do not always reflect reality. “HPC vendors typically quote performance numbers that represent the absolute fastest their machine can operate. We refer to that as the theoretical peak performance,” Sorensen explains. “The true benchmark of an effective system design is its ability to perform optimally on the specific tasks it is intended for. In fact, it’s common for some HPCs to achieve less than 25 percent of their alleged peak performance when handling real-world applications.” Thus, the genuine value of supercomputers lies in the actual work they accomplish, not merely their theoretical peak performance. For Meta, this work involves developing moderation systems at a time when trust in the company is critically low and creating a groundbreaking computing platform — whether through augmented reality glasses or the metaverse — that it can dominate against competitors like Google, Microsoft, and Apple. An AI supercomputer provides the raw power, but Meta must devise the winning strategy independently.

"The chip executes 660 operations per cycle, achieving an impressive 230 gigaflops at 350 MHz, according to Gupta. AI supercomputers are expertly constructed by integrating multiple graphic processing units (GPUs) into compute nodes, all interconnected through a high-performance network fabric for rapid communication among the GPUs, as highlighted by Meta in their blog. The supercomputers market is dominated by a select group of major players who command a significant share. Notable contributors include HPE, Atos SE, Dell Inc., Fujitsu Corporation, IBM Corporation, Lenovo Inc., and NEC Technologies India Private Limited, as reported by Mordor Intelligence. The firm forecasts robust growth in the supercomputers market, estimating an increase of approximately 9.5% from 2022 to 2027. Additionally, the rise of cloud technology is identified as a pivotal trend, with supercomputing centers increasingly adopting cloud solutions to manage their growing workloads. The demand for data centers, AI, and machine learning is surging among enterprises, including government and educational institutions, driven in part by the COVID-19 pandemic's acceleration of supercomputer demand, as stated in a report by Mordor Intelligence."

Meta has been a frontrunner in long-term investment in AI since 2013, when we established the Facebook AI Research lab. Our advancements in AI have been remarkable, driven by our expertise in key areas such as self-supervised learning, where algorithms effectively harness vast amounts of unlabeled data, and transformers, which enhance AI models' reasoning capabilities by focusing on critical input segments. To unlock the full potential of self-supervised learning and transformer-based models, various fields—ranging from vision and speech to language and essential applications like detecting harmful content—must adopt increasingly large, sophisticated, and adaptable models. For instance, computer vision requires the ability to process longer videos at higher data sampling rates, while speech recognition must excel in noisy environments like parties or concerts. Natural Language Processing (NLP) is tasked with comprehending a wider array of languages, dialects, and accents. Moreover, progress in robotics, embodied AI, and multimodal AI will empower individuals to achieve meaningful tasks in real-world scenarios. The backbone of training these expansive models is high-performance computing infrastructure, which Meta’s AI research team has been meticulously developing for years. Our first-generation infrastructure, designed in 2017, boasts 22,000 NVIDIA V100 Tensor Core GPUs within a single cluster, capable of executing 35,000 training jobs daily.

At that point, the system will boast approximately 16,000 GPUs, enabling it to train AI models with over a trillion parameters on data sets as large as an exabyte. While this impressive count of GPUs offers a limited perspective on overall performance, it's worth noting that Microsoft’s AI supercomputer, developed in collaboration with research lab OpenAI, utilizes 10,000 GPUs for comparison. These figures underscore the capabilities of AI supercomputers, but they raise a crucial question: what exactly defines an AI supercomputer? How does it stack up against the traditional supercomputers typically employed by universities and governments to tackle complex challenges in fields such as space exploration, nuclear physics, and climate science? Both categories, referred to as high-performance computers (HPCs), share more similarities than differences. They resemble datacenters more than individual machines and depend on vast networks of interconnected processors to exchange data at incredibly high speeds. However, as HPC analyst Bob Sorensen from Hyperion Research articulates to The Verge, there are significant distinctions. “AI-based HPCs operate in a distinctly different realm than their traditional HPC counterparts,” Sorensen asserts, emphasizing that the primary difference lies in accuracy.

Google has developed the TPU, or tensor processing unit, specifically designed to train large generative AI systems such as PaLM-2 and Imagen. Amazon provides its AWS customers with proprietary chips for both training (Trainium) and inferencing (Inferentia). Microsoft is actively collaborating with AMD to create its own AI chip, named Athena. Meta introduced the first generation of the MTIA — MTIA v1 — in 2020, utilizing a 7-nanometer process. This chip can scale from its internal 128 MB of memory to an impressive 128 GB. In a benchmark test devised by Meta, the MTIA demonstrated superior efficiency in handling “low-complexity” and “medium-complexity” AI models compared to a GPU. Although Meta acknowledges the need for further advancements in memory and networking to eliminate bottlenecks as AI model sizes increase, it is making strategic moves to address these challenges, including the acquisition of a team from Graphcore focused on AI networking technology. Currently, the MTIA is dedicated to inference tasks for “recommendation workloads” within Meta’s app ecosystem.

AI supercomputers and traditional supercomputers cannot be directly compared. Simply put, machine learning tasks demand less precision than those assigned to conventional supercomputers. Consequently, “AI supercomputers” — a term that has gained traction recently — are capable of executing more calculations per second than their traditional counterparts, even when utilizing the same hardware. Thus, when Meta claims to have constructed the “world’s fastest AI supercomputer,” it is important to recognize that this assertion doesn’t equate to the supercomputers frequently highlighted in the media, whose rankings are independently compiled by Top500.org and published biannually. To clarify further, both supercomputers and AI supercomputers perform calculations using floating-point arithmetic, a mathematical approach that effectively handles very large and very small numbers. The "floating point" refers to the decimal point's ability to shift between significant figures. The accuracy of floating-point calculations can be adjusted according to various formats, with the speed of most supercomputers measured in terms of 64-bit floating-point operations per second (FLOPs). However, since AI computations require less accuracy, AI supercomputers are typically evaluated using 32-bit or even 16-bit FLOPs.

It shattered records as the fastest film to surpass the $600 million milestone, outpacing The Force Awakens by achieving this in just 11 days—less than half the 26 days Infinity War required. The following week, it secured an impressive $64.8 million, marking the fourth-best third weekend in history. It swiftly crossed the $700 million threshold, tying The Force Awakens’s record of 16 days. Endgame was ultimately dethroned in its fourth weekend by the newcomer John Wick: Chapter 3 – Parabellum (2019). The film generated $17.2 million the subsequent weekend, totaling $22.3 million over the four-day Memorial Day weekend, while comfortably exceeding the $800 million mark domestically. During its re-release in the tenth weekend, it expanded to 1,040 theaters and achieved $6.1 million, a remarkable 207% increase from the previous weekend. In its thirteenth weekend, it earned $1.2 million, pushing it past Avatar's all-time record. Internationally, Endgame was projected to amass around $680 million in its first five days, leading to a global debut estimated between $850 and $950 million. Initially, it was expected to gross $250–280 million in China for its opening weekend, but it astonishingly made a record $107 million.

The film boasts an impressive array of nominations, including an Annie Award, which it secured, a British Academy Film Award, and three Critics' Choice Movie Awards, winning two of those. It also achieved the remarkable distinction of being the most-viewed Wikipedia article of 2019, racking up an astonishing 44.2 million page views. In February 2022, the iconic Avengers assembling moment was rightfully recognized as one of the five finalists for the Oscars Cheer Moment during the "Oscars Fan Favorite" contest for the 94th Academy Awards, even though the film was ineligible for other Academy Awards that year. Furthermore, in May 2018, Disney CEO Bob Iger confidently stated that Marvel intends to explore new franchises beyond the Avengers, while also indicating that more Avengers films could certainly be on the horizon. "Given the popularity of the characters and the franchise," he remarked, "I don't think people should conclude there will never be another Avengers movie." Shortly after the film premiered, the Russo brothers expressed their openness to returning to the Marvel Cinematic Universe in the future, thanks to their strong relationship with Marvel Studios, although they had no immediate plans. Additionally, in January 2021, Marvel Studios President Kevin Feige confirmed that another Avengers film is indeed on the way "at some point."

Elden Ring features a robust multiplayer system that enables players to be summoned for both cooperative gameplay and player-versus-player (PvP) battles online. In cooperative play, players strategically place a summon sign on the ground, making it visible to others who possess a matching item. When another player interacts with this sign, the summoner is drawn into their world. Cooperative players remain together until they conquer the area's boss or until a summoned player falls and returns home. In PvP scenarios, a summon sign can initiate a duel, or players can employ additional items to invade others' worlds. World hosts can utilize a "taunter's tongue" to enhance the chances of being invaded and to minimize the waiting time between invasions. Set in the Lands Between, a fictional realm ruled by several demigods, Elden Ring was once governed by the immortal Queen Marika, the caretaker of the Elden Ring—a formidable force representing order. Following Marika's shattering of the Elden Ring and her subsequent disappearance, her demigod offspring began to battle for its fragments in a conflict known as The Shattering. Each demigod wields a shard of the Ring, referred to as a Great Rune, which imbues them with overwhelming power.

In this game, you step into the role of a Tarnished, a member of a group of exiles from the Lands Between, summoned back into action following the Shattering. Your mission is clear: navigate the realm, repair the Elden Ring, and ascend as the Elden Lord. Early in your quest, you encounter Melina, a maiden who steps forward to assist you since you are maidenless. She grants you the ability to convert runes into strength and presents you with a steed named Torrent. In return, she requests that you escort her to the Erdtree, the sacred home of the Elden Ring. Melina then leads you to the Roundtable Hold, a strategic meeting point for other Tarnished who share your goal of mending the Elden Ring. Here, the Hold’s benefactor, the Two Fingers, sets you on a path to collect the Great Runes and deliver them to the Erdtree for the restoration of the Elden Ring. You venture into the Lands Between, exploring its diverse locales and overcoming the demigods that stand in your way. After successfully acquiring at least two Great Runes, the Two Fingers grants you the opportunity to confront Morgott the Grace-Given, the demigod safeguarding the Erdtree. You emerge victorious against Morgott, yet you find a daunting wall of thorns obstructing your path to the interior of the Erdtree.

Marika's progeny, all demigods, seized the shards of the Elden Ring known as the Great Runes, and the madness stemming from their newfound power ignited a war: The Shattering. This conflict resulted in the abandonment by the Greater Will. Now, the grace of guidance will be bestowed upon the Tarnished, those who were forsaken by the grace of gold and cast out from the Lands Between. You, the living dead, with grace long forgotten, follow the path to the Lands Between beyond the fog-laden sea and stand before the Elden Ring. Claim your destiny and become the Elden Lord. Embark on a journey through the Lands Between, a remarkable fantasy world meticulously crafted by Hidetaka Miyazaki, the visionary behind the acclaimed DARK SOULS series, and George R. R. Martin, the bestselling author of A Song of Ice and Fire. Unravel the secrets of the Elden Ring’s immense power. Confront adversaries with rich backstories, engage with characters driven by their own distinct motivations, and face formidable creatures. ELDEN RING boasts expansive, fantastical landscapes and intricate, shadowy dungeons that intertwine flawlessly.

The Tarnished can achieve one of six distinct endings based on their actions throughout the game, including ascending to the role of Elden Lord, witnessing the destruction of the Elden Ring by Ranni the Witch, or wielding the Frenzied Flame to obliterate the Lands Between.
